labs.title: The Port That Should Not Be Open
labs.description: A workstation generated a brief cluster of process-access, registry, DNS, and Windows Filtering Platform events. Determine whether an unauthorized listener was established.
labs.hypothesis: PowerShell activity on a user workstation resulted in an unexpected HTTP listener being exposed to the network.

steps.title: Scope the affected asset
steps.prompt: Which hostname recorded the suspicious listener activity?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM http_listener_events WHERE "Hostname" IS NOT NULL AND lower("Message") LIKE '%listen%'
hints: Start with distinct values in the Hostname column.

steps.title: Identify the exposed port
steps.prompt: Which local source port was opened for inbound listening? Enter the number only.
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM http_listener_events WHERE "SourcePort" IS NOT NULL AND lower("Message") LIKE '%local port%'
hints: Windows Filtering Platform auditing records describe both binds and listeners.

steps.title: Name the confirming event
steps.prompt: Which Windows Event ID explicitly states that an application or service listened for incoming connections?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM http_listener_events WHERE lower("Message") LIKE '%listen on a port%'
hints: Compare the Message field for the Filtering Platform events near the bind.

steps.title: Resolve the listening application
steps.prompt: Which application name did Windows Security associate with the port 8000 listener?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM http_listener_events WHERE "SourcePort" = 8000 AND lower("Application") = 'system'
hints: Pivot from SourcePort 8000 to the Application field in events 5154 and 5158.

steps.title: Correlate the event provider
steps.prompt: Which SourceName supplied the Windows Security listener event?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM http_listener_events WHERE "EventID" = 5154 AND lower("SourceName") = 'microsoft-windows-security-auditing'
hints: Compare the Sysmon context with the SourceName on the confirming Security event.

labs.title: A Transfer Hiding in Plain Sight
labs.description: Correlate Windows Security process auditing with Sysmon process, image-load, DNS, network, and file-create telemetry around BITSAdmin.
labs.hypothesis: A user abused the signed BITSAdmin utility to retrieve a PowerShell artifact into a temporary directory.

steps.title: Scope the transfer host
steps.prompt: Which hostname ran the suspicious BITSAdmin transfer?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM bitsadmin_events WHERE lower("Hostname") = 'workstation5' AND lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%bitsadmin%/transfer%'
hints: Filter process creation by CommandLine and inspect Hostname.

steps.title: Attribute the transfer
steps.prompt: Which local username launched BITSAdmin?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM bitsadmin_events WHERE "EventID" = 4688 AND lower("SubjectUserName") = 'wardog' AND lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%bitsadmin%'
hints: Windows Security EventID 4688 contains the creator subject.

steps.title: Correlate the Sysmon process
steps.prompt: What decimal ProcessId did Sysmon assign to bitsadmin.exe?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM bitsadmin_events WHERE "EventID" = 1 AND CAST("ProcessId" AS VARCHAR) = '9260' AND lower("Image") LIKE '%bitsadmin.exe'
hints: Match the Security process creation with Sysmon EventID 1.

steps.title: Recover the downloaded artifact
steps.prompt: What filename was written beneath the user's Temp directory?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM bitsadmin_events WHERE lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%bitsadmin1_flag.ps1%'
hints: Read the final argument in the BITSAdmin command line.

steps.title: Confirm the audit source
steps.prompt: Which Windows Security Event ID records the BITSAdmin process creation?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM bitsadmin_events WHERE "EventID" = 4688 AND lower("SourceName") = 'microsoft-windows-security-auditing' AND lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%bitsadmin%'
hints: Correlate the Sysmon EventID 1 record with its Windows Security equivalent.

labs.title: The Stylesheet That Executed
labs.description: Correlate Windows Security process auditing with Sysmon parent-child telemetry to expose WMIC XSL script processing.
labs.hypothesis: WMIC retrieved a remote XSL file and used its embedded script to spawn a child process on a workstation.

steps.title: Scope the endpoint
steps.prompt: Which hostname executed the remote XSL command?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM wmic_xsl_events WHERE lower("Hostname") = 'workstation5' AND lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%wmicscript.xsl%'
hints: Filter CommandLine for /FORMAT and inspect Hostname.

steps.title: Attribute the command
steps.prompt: Which local username launched WMIC?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM wmic_xsl_events WHERE "EventID" = 4688 AND lower("SubjectUserName") = 'wardog' AND lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%wmicscript.xsl%'
hints: Windows Security EventID 4688 identifies the creator subject.

steps.title: Recover the remote artifact
steps.prompt: What was the filename of the remote XSL stylesheet?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM wmic_xsl_events WHERE lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%wmicscript.xsl%'
hints: Read the final path segment of the URL supplied to /FORMAT.

steps.title: Identify the payload
steps.prompt: Which child executable did WMIC launch? Enter the filename only.
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM wmic_xsl_events WHERE "EventID" = 1 AND lower("Image") LIKE '%calc.exe' AND lower("ParentImage") LIKE '%wmic.exe'
hints: Use Sysmon process-create events and correlate ParentImage with Image.

steps.title: Confirm with Sysmon
steps.prompt: Which Sysmon Event ID records both the WMIC process and its child process creation?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM wmic_xsl_events WHERE "EventID" = 1 AND lower("Channel") = 'microsoft-windows-sysmon/operational' AND lower(COALESCE("CommandLine",'')) LIKE '%wmicscript.xsl%'
hints: Compare Windows Security 4688 with the matching Sysmon event type.

labs.title: Hands on the Credential Vault
labs.description: Correlate Sysmon process access with Windows Security object auditing to reconstruct credential access against LSASS.
labs.hypothesis: An interactive PowerShell process accessed LSASS memory on a workstation in a pattern consistent with credential dumping.

steps.title: Scope the attacked endpoint
steps.prompt: Which fully qualified hostname contains the suspicious PowerShell-to-LSASS access?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM mimikatz_events WHERE lower("Hostname") = 'workstation5.theshire.local' AND "EventID" = 10 AND lower("SourceImage") LIKE '%powershell.exe' AND lower("TargetImage") LIKE '%lsass.exe'
hints: Filter Sysmon EventID 10 and inspect SourceImage, TargetImage, and Hostname together.

steps.title: Attribute the Security audit
steps.prompt: Which username requested a handle to the LSASS process? Enter the username without a domain.
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM mimikatz_events WHERE "EventID" IN (4656,4663) AND lower("ObjectName") LIKE '%lsass.exe' AND lower("SubjectUserName") = 'pgustavo'
hints: Windows Security events 4656 and 4663 identify the subject separately from Sysmon.

steps.title: Identify the accessing process
steps.prompt: What decimal SourceProcessId did Sysmon record for the suspicious PowerShell process?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM mimikatz_events WHERE "EventID" = 10 AND CAST("SourceProcessId" AS VARCHAR) = '1648' AND lower("TargetImage") LIKE '%lsass.exe'
hints: Find the EventID 10 row where SourceImage is PowerShell and TargetImage is LSASS.

steps.title: Resolve the LSASS process
steps.prompt: What decimal TargetProcessId belonged to LSASS in that Sysmon process-access event?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM mimikatz_events WHERE "EventID" = 10 AND CAST("TargetProcessId" AS VARCHAR) = '768' AND lower("SourceImage") LIKE '%powershell.exe'
hints: Correlate the same Sysmon row using SourceProcessId 1648.

steps.title: Correlate the requested rights
steps.prompt: Which hexadecimal GrantedAccess mask did Sysmon record for PowerShell reading LSASS?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM mimikatz_events WHERE "EventID" = 10 AND lower("GrantedAccess") = '0x1010' AND lower("SourceImage") LIKE '%powershell.exe' AND lower("TargetImage") LIKE '%lsass.exe'
hints: Confirm the mask against the Windows Security handle request describing process-memory access.

labs.title: Service Across the Wire
labs.description: Two Windows workstations show a dense burst of SMB, named-pipe, service-control, and process activity. Reconstruct the remote execution sequence.
labs.hypothesis: An operator used SMBExec-style remote service creation to move from one workstation to another and launch a PowerShell stager as SYSTEM.

steps.title: Locate the destination
steps.prompt: Which fully qualified hostname received the remotely created service?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM smbexec_events WHERE "Hostname" IS NOT NULL AND "EventID" IN (4697, 7045)
hints: Filter service-installation events 4697 and 7045, then inspect Hostname.

steps.title: Find the RPC control path
steps.prompt: Which named-pipe target was accessed immediately before service creation?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM smbexec_events WHERE "EventID" = 5145 AND "RelativeTargetName" IS NOT NULL
hints: Event 5145 records detailed share access; inspect RelativeTargetName on IPC$.

steps.title: Recover the transient service name
steps.prompt: What randomized service name was installed on the destination?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM smbexec_events WHERE "ServiceName" IS NOT NULL AND "EventID" IN (4697, 7045)
hints: Service creation events expose ServiceName even when the command is encoded.

steps.title: Attribute the remote action
steps.prompt: Which account created the remote service? Enter the username without a domain.
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM smbexec_events WHERE "SubjectUserName" IS NOT NULL AND "EventID" IN (5140, 5145, 4697)
hints: Correlate the share-access and service-installation records by time and user.

steps.title: Confirm service installation
steps.prompt: Which System log Event ID confirms that the remote service was installed?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM smbexec_events WHERE "EventID" = 7045 AND "ServiceName" IS NOT NULL
hints: Correlate Security event 4697 with the Service Control Manager record in the System channel.

labs.title: The Trusted Binary Detour
labs.description: Follow a Regsvr32 scriptlet from PowerShell logging and Security auditing into Sysmon process, module, and network evidence.
labs.hypothesis: A signed Windows utility proxied execution of a remote scriptlet and launched an encoded PowerShell stager.

steps.title: Locate the proxy execution
steps.prompt: Which fully qualified hostname executed the suspicious Regsvr32 command?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM regsvr32_events WHERE lower("Hostname") = 'workstation5.mordor.local' AND lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%launcher.sct%'
hints: Filter CommandLine for regsvr32 and inspect Hostname.

steps.title: Attribute the operator
steps.prompt: Which username launched Regsvr32? Enter the username without a domain.
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM regsvr32_events WHERE "EventID" = 4688 AND lower("SubjectUserName") = 'pgustavo' AND lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%regsvr32%'
hints: Use the Windows Security process-create event rather than the SYSTEM account on Sysmon collection records.

steps.title: Recover the scriptlet
steps.prompt: What remote scriptlet filename was supplied to the /i switch?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM regsvr32_events WHERE lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%/i:%launcher.sct%'
hints: Read the URL path following the /i switch.

steps.title: Identify the scriptlet engine
steps.prompt: Which DLL name was passed as the final Regsvr32 argument?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM regsvr32_events WHERE lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%scrobj.dll%' AND lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%regsvr32%'
hints: The COM scriptlet engine DLL is the final token in the command.

steps.title: Correlate PowerShell logging
steps.prompt: Which PowerShell Operational Event ID captured the decoded Regsvr32 command as script-block text?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM regsvr32_events WHERE "EventID" = 4104 AND lower("SourceName") = 'microsoft-windows-powershell' AND lower("Message") LIKE '%launcher.sct%'
hints: Pivot from Sysmon and Security to the PowerShell Operational channel.

labs.title: A Task Built to Survive
labs.description: Trace a scheduled task from PowerShell staging through Sysmon process and registry telemetry to Task Scheduler registration.
labs.hypothesis: An operator established elevated persistence by registering an on-logon task that runs a hidden PowerShell payload as SYSTEM.

steps.title: Locate the persistence host
steps.prompt: Which fully qualified hostname registered the suspicious task?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM scheduled_task_events WHERE lower("Hostname") = 'workstation5.theshire.local' AND lower("TaskName") LIKE '%mordorelevated%'
hints: Start with Task Scheduler Operational events and inspect Hostname.

steps.title: Name the task
steps.prompt: What task name was registered? Enter it without the leading backslash.
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM scheduled_task_events WHERE lower("TaskName") LIKE '%mordorelevated%'
hints: TaskName is parsed on Task Scheduler events 106, 140, and 141.

steps.title: Identify the run-as identity
steps.prompt: Which account was configured to run the elevated task?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM scheduled_task_events WHERE lower("TaskName") LIKE '%mordorelevated%' AND lower("AccountName") = 'system'
hints: Correlate the /RU value in the creation command with Task Scheduler's AccountName.

steps.title: Recover the trigger
steps.prompt: Which schedule token followed /SC in the schtasks command?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM scheduled_task_events WHERE lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%/sc onlogon%' AND lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%mordorelevated%'
hints: Inspect the Sysmon process-create CommandLine for schtasks.exe.

steps.title: Confirm scheduler registration
steps.prompt: Which Task Scheduler Operational Event ID confirms the task registration?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM scheduled_task_events WHERE "EventID" = 106 AND lower("SourceName") = 'microsoft-windows-taskscheduler' AND lower("TaskName") LIKE '%mordorelevated%'
hints: Pivot from Sysmon process and registry events to the Task Scheduler Operational channel.

labs.title: An Account Across the Wire
labs.description: Follow a remote WMIC command from PowerShell and Sysmon telemetry on the source to WMI-hosted account creation on the destination.
labs.hypothesis: An operator used WMIC for lateral movement and created a local backdoor account on a second workstation.

steps.title: Locate the source endpoint
steps.prompt: Which fully qualified hostname launched the remote WMIC command?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM wmic_remote_events WHERE lower("Hostname") = 'workstation5.theshire.local' AND lower("Image") LIKE '%wmic.exe' AND lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%net user%'
hints: Find the Sysmon WMIC process-create event and inspect Hostname.

steps.title: Identify the remote target
steps.prompt: Which short hostname followed WMIC's /node switch?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM wmic_remote_events WHERE lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%/node:workstation6%'
hints: Read the /node value in the source-side WMIC CommandLine.

steps.title: Attribute the operator
steps.prompt: Which domain username launched the source-side WMIC process? Enter the username only.
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM wmic_remote_events WHERE lower("User") LIKE '%\pgustavo' AND lower("Image") LIKE '%wmic.exe'
hints: The Sysmon User field contains domain and username.

steps.title: Recover the created account
steps.prompt: What local username did the destination-side net.exe command attempt to add?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM wmic_remote_events WHERE lower("Hostname") = 'workstation6.theshire.local' AND lower("CommandLine") LIKE '%net user%backdoor%'
hints: Pivot to WORKSTATION6 and inspect net.exe and net1.exe command lines.

steps.title: Prove WMI parentage
steps.prompt: Which parent executable spawned net.exe on the destination? Enter the filename only.
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM wmic_remote_events WHERE "EventID" = 1 AND lower("ParentImage") LIKE '%wmiprvse.exe' AND lower("Image") LIKE '%net.exe'
hints: The destination Sysmon event reveals the WMI provider host as ParentImage.

labs.title: The Session on Port 5985
labs.description: Correlate WinRM host execution across Security, Sysmon, and PowerShell with packet-level source, destination, port, and flow evidence.
labs.hypothesis: A remote operator used PowerShell Remoting over WinRM to execute commands on a second workstation.

steps.title: Identify the remote endpoint
steps.prompt: Which fully qualified hostname created the WinRM provider host process?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM psremoting_host_events WHERE lower("Hostname") = 'workstation6.theshire.local' AND "EventID" = 1 AND lower("Image") LIKE '%wsmprovhost.exe'
hints: Find the Sysmon process-create event for the WinRM provider host.

steps.title: Attribute the remote session
steps.prompt: Which username owns the wsmprovhost.exe process? Enter the username only.
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM psremoting_host_events WHERE "EventID" = 1 AND lower("User") LIKE '%\pgustavo' AND lower("Image") LIKE '%wsmprovhost.exe'
hints: The Sysmon User field includes both domain and username.

steps.title: Name the provider process
steps.prompt: Which executable hosts the remote PowerShell runspace? Enter the filename only.
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM psremoting_host_events WHERE "EventID" = 1 AND lower("Image") LIKE '%wsmprovhost.exe' AND lower("Hostname") = 'workstation6.theshire.local'
hints: Inspect the process description and Image on the destination host.

steps.title: Pivot to the network table
steps.prompt: Which destination TCP port carries the dominant WinRM flow?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM psremoting_network WHERE "Protocol" = 'TCP' AND "DestinationPort" = 5985
hints: Query psremoting_network and group by DestinationPort.

steps.title: Identify the initiating address
steps.prompt: Which source IP initiated the WinRM connections to destination port 5985?
steps.verification_sql: SELECT count(*) FROM psremoting_network WHERE "SourceIp" = '172.18.39.5' AND "DestinationIp" = '172.18.39.6' AND "DestinationPort" = 5985
hints: Filter the packet table to destination port 5985, then compare the directional IP pair.
